case_name: Diuron removal from well water for drinking water production with minimal chemical use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for diuron-contaminated well water intended for drinking water supply under a minimal-chemical-use operating preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A small groundwater-based drinking water utility has detected the herbicide diuron in a production well influenced by nearby agricultural activity. The utility wants a reliable and established treatment approach that can reduce pesticide concentrations for potable use while keeping chemical addition to a minimum. Existing staff can operate standard filtration and adsorption systems but prefer to avoid complex multi-chemical treatment.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of diuron to comply with applicable drinking water expectations for pesticide control and maintain stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven treatment technology for trace organic micropollutants
maintain low to moderate operational complexity

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: remove incidental particles and protect downstream adsorptive media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb diuron and other trace hydrophobic organic compounds from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and polish finished water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: diuron is the single clearly identified contaminant and activated carbon is a standard barrier for many pesticide and micropollutant removals in drinking water treatment
the well water has low turbidity and modest DOC, which supports effective carbon adsorption and reasonable bed life
this approach aligns strongly with the minimal_chemical_use constraint because routine removal is achieved primarily by adsorption rather than chemical oxidation or coagulation
operation is familiar and robust for small to medium drinking water facilities
potential_risks: carbon breakthrough can occur if empty bed contact time and replacement frequency are not properly managed
natural organic matter can gradually compete for adsorption sites and shorten media life
spent carbon handling or regeneration logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: stabilize influent solids loading before adsorption contact
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: adsorb diuron during controlled contact time
core_unit_functions.solid_liquid_separation: remove spent powdered carbon and associated solids
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished water

applicability_rationale: activated carbon remains a proven treatment principle for pesticide removal
PAC can be effective when flexible dosing is desired during seasonal or variable contamination episodes
the process is established in drinking water treatment, but it relies more on recurring material addition than fixed-bed GAC
this option is technically sound but slightly less aligned with the stated desire to minimize chemical or consumable inputs
potential_risks: requires regular PAC dosing control and reliable solids removal
carbon slurry handling adds operator burden compared with fixed-bed GAC
incorrect dose or contact time may lead to inconsistent diuron removal

core_unit_functions.uv_based_advanced_oxidation: oxidatively transform diuron and other susceptible organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon_or_gac: remove oxidation byproducts and provide polishing adsorption

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade certain pesticide molecules and may be considered where a destruction step is preferred
a downstream carbon stage is commonly used to polish transformation products and add robustness
the train is technically capable but less preferred because it usually needs oxidant addition and tighter process control
it is therefore weaker against the minimal_chemical_use objective than direct adsorption
potential_risks: advanced oxidation can create transformation byproducts that still require downstream control
performance depends on UV dose, oxidant management, and water matrix effects
higher equipment complexity and power demand may be unnecessary for a single low-level contaminant case

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: physically reject dissolved organic micropollutants including diuron
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: pressure membranes can provide strong barrier performance for many dissolved contaminants including pesticides
this option may be considered if future broader contaminant removal becomes necessary
for the present single-contaminant well water case, the process is more energy intensive and operationally demanding than needed
it does not fit the minimal_chemical_use and moderate complexity preference as well as activated carbon
potential_risks: concentrate management creates an additional waste stream
membrane fouling and scaling control may require pretreatment and chemical cleaning

core_unit_functions.conventional_coagulation: destabilize colloids and form settleable flocs

applicability_rationale: this is a well-known drinking water process train but it is not a strong standalone solution for dissolved pesticide removal at trace concentrations
diuron is the explicit treatment target and conventional solids removal processes do not directly address it effectively
the train also uses routine chemical addition, making it poorly aligned with the minimal-chemical-use preference
it ranks as a low-priority fallback only because it is conventional, not because it is well matched to the contaminant
potential_risks: insufficient diuron removal is likely without an added adsorptive or oxidative barrier
chemical sludge production increases residuals handling requirements
selection of this train alone could fail the core treatment objective

must_reflect: minimal routine chemical addition is a primary preference
established drinking water technologies should be favored over unnecessarily complex systems
high-energy membrane solutions should rank below adsorption unless broader treatment needs are stated

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for pesticide or micropollutant removal
why low turbidity and modest DOC support adsorption performance
why advanced oxidation or membranes rank lower under the stated constraints
why conventional clarification alone is not the preferred barrier for dissolved diuron

should_mention: carbon breakthrough and media replacement needs
possible oxidation byproducts if advanced oxidation is proposed
concentrate or cleaning burdens for membrane options
